title: What Is Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)?
choices: To prevent a Layer 2 broadcast loop that would flood the network
To save electricity on unused switch ports
Because switches can only have one active uplink by hardware design
To force all traffic through the fastest link only

content.explanation: Switches are often cabled with redundant links between them for resilience — but at Layer 2 there's no TTL to stop a frame, so a loop between two switches would circulate broadcast/unknown-unicast traffic forever, doubling and redoubling until the network melts down. Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) prevents this by calculating a loop-free tree and putting redundant ports into a 'blocking' state, ready to activate only if the active path fails.
content.example:
show spanning-tree vlan 10
Interface           Role Sts  Cost  Prio.Nbr
Gi0/1               Root FWD  4     128.1
Gi0/2               Altn BLK  4     128.2   <- redundant link, blocked to prevent a loop

check.statement: Why does STP block one of two redundant links between switches instead of using both?

acceptable_answers: to prevent a layer 2 broadcast loop that would flood the network
solution.explanation: Without STP, a redundant Layer 2 path creates a loop with no hop-count/TTL to stop it, so broadcast and unknown-unicast frames circulate and multiply endlessly. STP blocks the redundant port to keep the topology loop-free, and only unblocks it automatically if the active path fails.
